question: [File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a YAML prototype pollution attack by supplying a crafted YAML file at a CONFIG_PATH-controlled location containing `__proto__` or `constructor` keys, causing yaml.load() + extend(true, config, doc) to corrupt the global config object and crash >=30% of REST API node processes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the silent error suppression in the catch block (logger.warn only) to supply a malformed YAML file that causes yaml.load() to throw a synchronous exception that is swallowed, leaving the config object in a partially-initialized state that causes downstream parseDbPoolConfig() to crash the process on startup?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: extend()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a YAML config file with deeply nested keys that causes extend(true, config, doc) to perform unbounded recursive merging, exhausting the call stack and crashing the Node.js process during startup, taking down the REST API node?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a YAML file with an anchor/alias bomb (billion laughs attack) at the CONFIG_PATH location, causing yaml.load() to expand exponentially large structures into memory, exhausting heap memory and crashing the REST API process?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseDbPoolConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the db.pool.maxConnections config value to an extremely large integer (e.g., via HIERO_MIRROR_REST_DB_POOL_MAXCONNECTIONS env var or YAML override) causing the database connection pool to exhaust all available PostgreSQL connections, starving >=30% of mirror node REST API instances from connecting to the database?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseDbPoolConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set db.pool.connectionTimeout to 1 (minimum valid value per parseInt check) via a crafted config, causing all new DB connection attempts to time out after 1ms, effectively making the REST API unable to serve any requests and shutting down >=30% of processing nodes functionally?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseDbPoolConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set db.pool.statementTimeout to 1 (minimum valid value) via config injection, causing all database queries to time out after 1ms, making every API request fail and rendering >=30% of REST API nodes non-functional for query processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseDurationConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an astronomically large duration string (e.g., '999999999999999999999d') for maxTimestampRange via YAML config, causing parseDuration() to return a value that when multiplied by NANOSECONDS_PER_MILLISECOND as BigInt overflows or produces an incorrect constraint, allowing unbounded timestamp range queries that exhaust DB resources across all nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseDurationConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set maxRecordFileCloseInterval to an extremely large duration value via config, causing the query planner to scan an unbounded number of time partitions per request, generating excessive DB I/O that degrades >=30% of mirror node processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseQueryConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted precedingTransactionTypes array with thousands of entries via YAML config, causing every transaction-by-account-id query to perform an IN clause with thousands of values, generating excessive DB load that degrades >=30% of network processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the case-insensitive property matching in setConfigValue() to override security-critical config values (e.g., db.pool.maxConnections) via environment variables with mixed-case names that bypass operator intent, causing resource exhaustion across >=30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: setConfigValue()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HEDERA_MIRROR to hiero.mirror automatic migration in setConfigValue() to inject duplicate config overrides that cause the last-write-wins behavior to set db.pool.maxConnections to an extreme value, exhausting DB connections across >=30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply the string 'Infinity' as a config value via environment variable, causing convertType() to return +Infinity (since !isNaN('Infinity') is true), which when used as db.pool.maxConnections creates an unbounded connection pool that exhausts all PostgreSQL connections?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply '0x7FFFFFFF' as a config value via environment variable, causing convertType() to return a large integer (since !isNaN('0x7FFFFFFF') is true in some JS engines), setting db.pool.maxConnections to an extreme value that exhausts DB connections across >=30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: convertType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply '1e308' as a config value via environment variable, causing convertType() to return Number.MAX_VALUE (since !isNaN('1e308') is true), which when used as db.pool.maxConnections or statementTimeout causes parseInt to produce MAX_SAFE_INTEGER-level values that exhaust DB resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: load()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that both application.yml and application.yaml are loaded sequentially without deduplication in load(), supplying both files at CONFIG_PATH with conflicting values that cause the second load to overwrite security-critical settings like db.pool.maxConnections to an extreme value?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: load()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CONFIG_PATH environment variable to point to a directory containing a crafted application.yml that sets db.pool.maxConnections=1, causing all REST API nodes sharing that config path to be limited to a single DB connection, effectively shutting down >=30% of processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadEnvironment()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that loadEnvironment() iterates ALL process.env entries and calls setConfigValue() on each, causing a performance degradation during startup if the environment contains thousands of variables, delaying or crashing node initialization across >=30% of instances?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseUsersConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of password hashing in parseUsersConfig() — passwords are stored in plaintext in the config object — to enumerate valid credentials via timing side-channels in tsscmp() comparisons, then authenticate as a high-limit user and issue queries that exhaust DB resources across >=30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseUsersConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that user.limit is set via parseInt(user.limit, 10) without an upper bound check in parseUsersConfig(), allowing a configured user with limit=Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER to request that many rows per query, generating DB queries that exhaust memory and crash >=30% of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: getResponseLimit()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getResponseLimit() returns a direct reference to the mutable config.response.limit object, allowing any code path that mutates this object to permanently alter response limits for all subsequent requests, potentially setting limit.max to an extreme value that causes DB exhaustion across >=30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a YAML file containing a JavaScript Date object or RegExp tag that causes yaml.load() (using DEFAULT_SCHEMA) to instantiate arbitrary JS objects, leading to unexpected type coercions in extend() that corrupt numeric config values like db.pool.maxConnections to NaN, causing parseDbPoolConfig() to throw and crash the process?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: loadYaml()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a YAML file with a circular reference structure that causes extend(true, config, doc) to enter an infinite loop during deep merge, hanging the Node.js event loop and making the REST API node unresponsive, effectively shutting it down?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an extremely large shard or realm value in the YAML config, causing parseCommon() to call BigInt() on a value that exceeds safe integer bounds, producing a BigInt that causes downstream query generation to produce invalid SQL, crashing >=30% of REST API nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/config.js -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: parseCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a non-numeric string as the shard value in YAML config, causing BigInt(common.shard) in parseCommon() to throw a TypeError that is not caught, crashing the REST API process during startup and taking down >=30% of nodes?
